metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC1 jq proving command to assert cardinality and name inline, e.g. pipe to `| if (length == 1 and .[0].name == "rebar-opcert-admin-invoke-policy") then "PASS" else error("unexpected invoke grants: \(.)" ) end` so the command exits non-zero on any deviation.
findings.finding: The jq proving command in AC1 produces an array but never asserts its cardinality or the name value inline — the plan prose says 'asserting the resulting array has length 1 and its name is the rebar-opcert-admin invoke policy' but the literal command shown omits that assertion step. A CI step running the command as written would silently pass even if the array were empty or contained unexpected entries, satisfying no guarantee.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, jq command block
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the bare jq expression with:
```bash
terraform plan -out=tf.plan && terraform show -json tf.plan \
  | jq -e '
    [ .planned_values.root_module.resources[]
      | select(.type == "aws_iam_role_policy" or .type == "aws_iam_policy")
      | {name: .name, s: (.values.policy | fromjson | .Statement[])}
      | select([.s.Action] | flatten | any(. == "execute-api:Invoke"))
    ]
    | if length == 1 and .[0].name == "rebar-opcert-admin-invoke-policy"
      then .
      else error("invoke-grant assertion failed: \(.)")
      end
  '
```
This makes the command exit 1 (and print the offending array) on any deviation from the expected single-role constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which nginx config file gains the `/opcert/` location block and the `include` directive for the guard-header conf fragment (e.g., `rebar.conf.template` gets an `include /etc/nginx/conf.d/opcert-guard.conf;` stanza, and the location block is added there), so AC3's nginx-route assertion and the materialize script's output target are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'HOST-nginx conf include' mechanism for the origin-guard value, but infra/nginx/ contains only rebar.conf.template with no include directive and no /opcert/ location block. The nginx conf include target, the include directive wiring it into the live server config, and the /opcert/ location block must all be added to rebar.conf.template (or a new file + include stanza) — but the plan never identifies rebar.conf.template as the file to modify or specifies where the include directive is inserted, leaving the 'HOST-nginx conf include' claim unbacked by the existing nginx configuration.
findings.location: What section / Compose — nginx wiring paragraph: 'nginx route /opcert/ → the service that rejects requests whose X-Opcert-Guard header does not match … materialized from its SSM parameter into a HOST-nginx conf include'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Compose/nginx wiring paragraph, replace 'a HOST-nginx conf include' with a concrete specification: the materialize script writes the guard value to `/etc/nginx/conf.d/opcert-guard.conf` (a `map` or `geo` block, or a simple variable assignment), `rebar.conf.template` is updated to add an `include /etc/nginx/conf.d/opcert-guard.conf;` directive and a new `location /opcert/ { … if ($http_x_opcert_guard != $opcert_guard_value) { return 403; } … }` block. AC3 should name these two files as the test targets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` from the origin-guard SSM parameter (or make it explicit that the lifecycle block applies ONLY to the Ed25519 key parameter), since the guard value is Terraform-managed via `random_password` and must stay in sync with the API Gateway integration header across all applies.
findings.finding: The origin-guard SSM parameter (/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard) is given lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] } but its value is Terraform-managed (sourced from random_password.result, not operator-seeded). This means if random_password is ever replaced (state taint, state loss, provider upgrade), Terraform updates the API Gateway static header to the new guard value but leaves the SSM parameter frozen at the old value. The materialize script reads from SSM, so nginx enforces the stale guard while API Gateway injects the new one — breaking every request until a manual SSM fix.
findings.location: What section — origin-guard provisioning path; also AC1 ('the two SecureString parameters ... guarded by lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }')
findings.suggested_fix: Do not add lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] } to the /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard SSM parameter. Only the Ed25519 key parameter (whose value is operator-seeded out-of-band) needs that guard. The guard SSM parameter's value should always track random_password.result so APIGW and nginx stay in sync. Separately, document that rotating the guard requires both a terraform apply (updates APIGW header) and re-running materialize-opcert-guard.sh (updates nginx).

findings.finding: AC1's proving command for IAM exclusivity ('ONLY statement with action execute-api:Invoke is the one attached to rebar-opcert-admin') is described only in prose. No concrete jq expression is given to (a) extract all IAM statements with that action from the tf.plan JSON and (b) assert their count is exactly 1. A test that checks existence of the correct statement but omits the cardinality check would pass even if a second Invoke-granting statement were introduced, making the security invariant unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 — structured assertion block
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: 'The cardinality assertion uses `jq -e` on the structured plan JSON: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e \"[.. | objects | select(.actions? and (.actions[] | contains(\\"execute-api:Invoke\\"))) ] | length == 1\"` — this MUST return exit 0; any additional Invoke statement causes exit 1.'

findings.finding: AC1 asserts 'no other principal is granted [execute-api:Invoke]' but provides no proving command or test approach for this negative assertion. Asserting an absence across all IAM policy documents in the Terraform configuration (inline policies, resource-based policies, any future additions) requires exhaustive scanning, not just verifying the one IaC-created role. With no described approach, an executor has no guidance on how to implement this check, and a test that only verifies the `rebar-opcert-admin` role's inline policy exists would silently pass even if a second policy inadvertently also granted Invoke.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'A conftest (or equivalent) policy test scans all `aws_iam_role_policy`, `aws_iam_policy_document`, and `aws_iam_role` resources in `infra/terraform/` and asserts that `execute-api:Invoke` appears only in the `rebar-opcert-admin` inline policy; the test fails if any other resource document contains this action.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a structured assertion (parallel to the existing jq block) that inspects `aws_iam_role.rebar-opcert-admin`'s `assume_role_policy` in the `terraform show -json` output and asserts its `Principal` contains exactly the ARNs from `opcert_admin_principal_arns` and nothing broader.
findings.finding: AC1's structured jq assertion verifies only the permission policy (that `execute-api:Invoke` appears in exactly one policy whose name is the admin-role policy), but does NOT structurally assert the trust policy — i.e., that the `aws_iam_role` resource's assume-role policy is restricted to `var.opcert_admin_principal_arns`. A passing jq result is compatible with a wide-open trust policy (e.g., trusting all AWS principals), leaving the core security invariant — 'a key nobody else can read' / access restricted to declared principals — without an end-state-observable proof.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, structured jq assertion block
findings.suggested_fix:
Extend AC1 with a second jq command, e.g.:
```
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type == "aws_iam_role" and .name == "rebar-opcert-admin") | .values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[] | .Principal]'
```
and assert the result contains only the expected ARN(s) with no wildcard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a structured assertion in AC1 (e.g., a second jq pipeline over `terraform show -json` that selects `aws_iam_role` with `name == "rebar-opcert-admin"` and verifies `assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[].Principal` contains only the principals derived from `opcert_admin_principal_arns`) to complete the security verification of the trust-policy restriction.
findings.finding: AC1 references the trust-policy restriction to `opcert_admin_principal_arns` as a security property but provides no structured assertion to verify it — the only STRUCTURED test command (the `jq` pipeline in AC1) checks for `execute-api:Invoke` in IAM policies, not that the `rebar-opcert-admin` role's trust policy is actually scoped to `opcert_admin_principal_arns` and nothing else.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, jq command block
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1's structured assertion block: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type=="aws_iam_role" and .name=="rebar-opcert-admin") | .values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[].Principal]'` — assert the result contains only the expected principals and has length equal to the number of entries in `opcert_admin_principal_arns`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and explicitly name the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` attribute and its required key format (e.g. `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard`) for injecting a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header, citing the Terraform AWS provider documentation or a working HCL snippet, so the implementer cannot silently misconfigure the inner perimeter.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that Terraform will inject the origin-guard value as a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header on the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, but never cites the exact Terraform attribute name or its required key format. The `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource's `request_parameters` attribute uses a non-obvious key syntax (keys must be prefixed with `append:`, `overwrite:`, or `remove:`, e.g. `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard`). No `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource exists anywhere in `infra/terraform/` (confirmed by search), so there is no codebase prior art to reference. A wrong or missing key prefix is silently accepted by `terraform apply` but the header is never injected — the inner origin-guard perimeter then silently does not enforce, with no deploy-time error signal.
findings.location: What section — 'maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'; AC1 — 'the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section or AC1: 'Use `aws_apigatewayv2_integration.request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }` (the `overwrite:` prefix is required by the AWS provider for static header injection on HTTP API integrations).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the Terraform HCL attribute and key-format for the static `X-Opcert-Guard` injection on the HTTP API v2 integration (e.g. `request_parameters = { "append:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = "$context.requestOverride.header.X-Opcert-Guard" }` or a static-value variant) so the executor has a verified, flag-level specification rather than discovering it from AWS docs.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'injecting a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration' but never states the Terraform HCL syntax for this on an HTTP API v2 integration. The mechanism (`request_parameters` map on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration`) uses a specific key syntax (e.g. `"append:header.X-Opcert-Guard"`) that differs from REST API v1 mapping templates and is a new, unverified integration surface in this codebase. The plan neither names the attribute nor the key-format convention, leaving the executor to discover it.
findings.location: What section: 'the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping'; AC1: 'the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` (or a new conf.d include file path) as an explicit edit target for the `/opcert/` location block and X-Opcert-Guard enforcement, and describe how the include is wired into the host nginx configuration.
findings.finding: infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template is an unenumerated consumer: the plan introduces a new nginx conf include for the /opcert/ route with X-Opcert-Guard enforcement, but the existing template has no `include` directive and is never named in the plan's What or Scope sections as requiring modification.
findings.location: What section — 'Compose — extend infra/compose/docker-compose.yml' / 'a HOST-nginx conf include'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `special = false` (and optionally a fixed `length` ≥ 32) on the `random_password` resource for the origin guard, so the generated value is safe to embed literally in nginx `if`-directive strings and in API Gateway static header parameter values without escaping or encoding mismatches.
findings.finding: The `random_password` resource for the origin guard is specified without a safe-character-set constraint (`special = false` or equivalent). Terraform's `random_password` defaults include special characters (e.g., `!`, `@`, `#`, `\`, `"`, `;`). When this value is materialised into a HOST nginx conf include — e.g., inside an `if ($http_x_opcert_guard != "<VALUE>")` directive — unescaped quote, backslash, or semicolon characters can corrupt the nginx configuration file or enable nginx-directive injection, causing either a silent service outage (nginx reload fails) or, in the worst case, a misconfigured guard that accepts all requests.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password`'; AC1 — 'the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Terraform declaration add `resource "random_password" "opcert_origin_guard" { length = 32; special = false }`. This guarantees the value is `[A-Za-z0-9]` only, safe for unquoted nginx string comparison and API Gateway header value injection, and still provides ~190 bits of entropy (far exceeding what's needed for a shared origin secret).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `validation` block to the `opcert_admin_principal_arns` variable asserting `length(var.opcert_admin_principal_arns) > 0` and that no element equals `"*"`, and document in the variable description that wildcard ARNs are prohibited, so the access posture is declared and machine-checked.
findings.finding: The `opcert_admin_principal_arns` variable that controls the `rebar-opcert-admin` trust policy has no declared validation constraint. An empty list produces a trust policy with no allowed principals (locking out all operators from the authenticated API endpoint), while a list containing `"*"` or an overly broad ARN pattern makes the `execute-api:Invoke` grant effectively public, silently defeating the SigV4 boundary. The plan declares the trust policy is 'limited to the principal ARNs supplied via `opcert_admin_principal_arns`' but does not state that the variable is validated, leaving its access posture undeclared.
findings.location: What section — 'an `aws_iam_role` `rebar-opcert-admin` … trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable `opcert_admin_principal_arns`'; AC1 structured assertion
findings.suggested_fix: In variables.tf: `variable "opcert_admin_principal_arns" { type = list(string); description = "IAM principal ARNs trusted to assume rebar-opcert-admin (non-empty; no wildcards)"; validation { condition = length(var.opcert_admin_principal_arns) > 0 && !contains(var.opcert_admin_principal_arns, "*"); error_message = "opcert_admin_principal_arns must be non-empty and must not contain wildcard principals." } }`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out/recovery procedure for the key-seeding step to 'Deploy + prove' (and reference it from AC5): at minimum, specify the steps to re-generate and re-seed the key if seeding fails, and describe the procedure for key rotation (re-run ssh-keygen, PutParameter, update trusted_environments.yaml, re-attest).
findings.finding: The key-seeding step ('the key exists nowhere else') is an acknowledged irreversible operation, but the plan provides no explicit back-out or recovery procedure — leaving the deploy story without a reversibility mechanism for its only truly irreversible step, as required by T4.
findings.location: What — Deploy + prove section; AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a key-rotation runbook (even a brief one) to the plan: how to generate a new key, update the SSM SecureString with `--overwrite`, restart the opcert compose service to re-read the new key, and update `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` with the new public key — so the shared-state lifecycle is complete from CREATE through RETIRE.
findings.finding: The Ed25519 private key (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`) has no defined rotation or retirement path. The `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` directive explicitly removes Terraform from key management after seeding, and the plan describes no procedure for key rotation (e.g., key compromise, periodic rotation), nor how consumers (the opcert service) tolerate a key swap without downtime.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString declaration; AC line 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` to the `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` SSM parameter declaration (matching the pattern on `aws_ebs_volume.data` and `aws_eip.gerrit`) to guard the sole copy of the irreplaceable signing key against accidental `terraform destroy`.
findings.finding: The SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` holds the ONLY copy of the irreplaceable Ed25519 private key (the plan states 'the key exists nowhere else') but no `prevent_destroy` lifecycle guard is specified for it. A `terraform destroy` or accidental resource removal would permanently delete the signing key, breaking all existing SSHSIG certificates that have been issued against it with no recovery path.
findings.location: What section: '…SSM Parameter Store SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` (declared with a placeholder `value` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`)…'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the new SSM parameter resource for `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, add:
```hcl
lifecycle {
  ignore_changes  = [value]
  prevent_destroy = true
}
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit rollback procedure: (a) the nginx conf change is backed up before the materialize script writes it (so `nginx -s reload` failure can be recovered by restoring the backup); (b) `terraform destroy -target=<api_gateway_resources>` is listed as the Terraform rollback; (c) the compose service can be removed by commenting out the `opcert` stanza and re-running compose — specify this sequence in the plan.
findings.finding: No rollback procedure is specified for the case where the deploy needs to be undone. The plan changes a running system: it adds a host-nginx route (via `nginx -s reload` on a process also serving Gerrit), adds a compose service, and creates API Gateway + SSM resources via Terraform. If the deploy is partially broken, there is no described sequence for reverting these changes safely.
findings.location: Scope section; What section — compose/nginx wiring; Deploy + prove section
findings.suggested_fix: Before `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` writes the nginx conf include, copy the existing include (or a known-good stub) to a `.bak` file; on `nginx -t` failure after writing, restore the backup and exit non-zero.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pointer (in AC bullet 4 or the What section) to the canonical doc/spec that defines the trusted_environments.yaml schema and era protocol (e.g., a link to the epic design doc, a schema comment in the YAML file itself, or a CLAUDE.md entry), so operators can self-serve when adding future environments.
findings.finding: The plan refers to '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml' format and the 'epic's era protocol' without pointing to where that protocol is documented, leaving future operators no reference for the required fields (public key, env_id, added_at_commit) or the process for adding a new trusted environment.
findings.location: What section — 'pin the environment's public key + env_id into .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml per the epic's era protocol'; Acceptance Criteria bullet 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a clearly-labelled section in CLAUDE.md / an ops runbook) documenting the origin-guard header contract: what it is, why it is the origin-protection mechanism instead of VPC isolation, how it is seeded (Terraform → SSM → materialize script → nginx reload), and how to rotate it — so the security invariant survives future nginx or API Gateway changes.
findings.finding: The origin-guard header mechanism — a shared-secret header injected by API Gateway and validated by host nginx to protect the EC2 origin — is a novel cross-component security contract that is not captured in any new ADR or design doc. ADR 0046/0047 (referenced in the plan) cover cost/isolation rationale, not this specific pattern. A future maintainer modifying nginx config or the API Gateway integration without awareness of this contract could silently break origin protection.
findings.location: What section — 'origin-guard header provisioning path' + nginx route description; Acceptance Criteria bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/XXXX-opcert-origin-guard.md` (next ADR number) that records: (1) context — no VPC link, direct HTTPS to EC2 origin; (2) decision — API Gateway injects a Terraform-generated random header; nginx checks it and rejects mismatches; (3) consequences — rotation requires re-running materialize-opcert-guard.sh + nginx -s reload; key material lives in SSM under the existing path grant. Reference this ADR from the Terraform resource comment and the nginx config include.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add retry/backoff (e.g., exponential back-off loop with a max-attempt cap) to `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`'s SSM call and specify the failure behavior (abort compose-up with non-zero exit, or warn-and-skip with a safe default) so boot-time SSM blips don't silently leave the service in a broken state.
findings.finding: The `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` script fetches from SSM at every boot and is a hard prerequisite before `docker compose up`, but no retry/backoff or failure-handling is specified. A transient SSM unavailability at boot time would leave the host nginx unreloaded (stale or absent guard header) and the compose stack unstarted, with no recovery path short of a manual re-run.
findings.location: What section — compose/nginx wiring; AC line 3 (`infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`)
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `aws ssm get-parameter` call in a retry loop (e.g., 5 attempts, 5 s back-off). On final failure, exit non-zero so `compose-up.sh` propagates the error and the operator is alerted rather than the system silently continuing without the guard materialized.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify at minimum: API Gateway access logging to a CloudWatch log group (free tier sufficient for low-volume traffic) and a CloudWatch alarm on 5xx error rate, plus structured stderr/stdout logging from the opcert compose service captured by Docker's json-file driver — so new failure points are observable without SSH.
findings.finding: No observability instrumentation is specified for the new API Gateway HTTP API or the opcert service — no CloudWatch access logs on the API Gateway, no structured application logs from the service, no alarm on 4xx/5xx rates, and no health-check endpoint. Operators have no visibility into authentication failures, integration errors, or service-down conditions.
findings.location: What section — entire plan; AC line 5 (operator-attested live checks)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the origin-guard value should be generated via `hashicorp/random` (new provider, 0 existing call-sites) or follow the codebase's established pattern — `openssl rand -hex 32 | aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` in an operator-run step + `value = "CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` in Terraform — which avoids a new provider dependency and matches all 12 existing SSM secrets in `ssm.tf`.
findings.finding: `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) is introduced as a new Terraform provider with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0 of 3), solely to generate the origin-guard value — while the codebase's established pattern for generating and seeding single-use secrets is `openssl rand -hex 32` + `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite`, as used directly in `infra/gerrit/service-user.sh` line 62. This adds a new provider lifecycle (init, lock-file pin, upgrade path) for a single value that could follow the same CHANGEME-placeholder + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` pattern already applied to all 12 parameters in `ssm.tf`.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password` — the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf`'
findings.suggested_fix: Declare `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` in `ssm.tf` with `value = "CHANGEME"` and `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` (matching every other secret). Document the one-time seed step in the deploy procedure as `openssl rand -hex 32 | xargs -I{} aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite --type SecureString --name /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard --value {}`, following the `service-user.sh` precedent. This eliminates the `hashicorp/random` provider entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as a consumer: decide whether `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` belongs in CONFIG_PATHS (detect-only signal) and whether `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` should be excluded from BOT_PATHS triggers.
findings.finding: autodeploy.sh is an unenumerated consumer with two impacts: (1) its BOT_PATHS watches docker-compose.yml, so any opcert-only change to docker-compose.yml will spuriously trigger a review-bot rebuild; (2) infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh is outside all three watch-path sets (BOT_PATHS, CONFIG_PATHS, OBS_PATHS), so a guard-rotation script update auto-deployed to the box would never be re-executed by autodeploy.
findings.location: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh lines 55–61 / What section — 'Compose — extend infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC4's `added_at_commit` field with the parent epic's authoritative 'Authoritative era anchor' schema (`added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position`): either update this leaf's AC4 to use the superseding field name, or (if the parent's two schema sections are genuinely in conflict) update the parent first and re-review.
findings.finding: The leaf's AC4 pins `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` using the field `added_at_commit` (a git SHA on main), but the parent epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' section explicitly SUPERSEDES the earlier 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' and mandates `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions, operator-stamped via `rebar trusted-env`). The leaf is using the old, superseded field name.
findings.location: AC4 — `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` pinning; parent epic section 'Authoritative era anchor (security finding #5, change 664 rejected — SUPERSEDES the era-comparison rule in "Trust config & era-validity protocol" above)'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, replace `added_at_commit` with `added_at_log_position` (a tickets-branch log position, stamped via `rebar trusted-env` at key-pinning time), matching the parent's 'Authoritative era anchor' schema. The test assertion should verify the field name is `added_at_log_position` and that it holds a valid tickets-branch commit SHA, not a main-branch SHA.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify or replace the `hashicorp/random` / `random_password` approach for the origin-guard value: explain why the established CHANGEME + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` + operator CLI seeding pattern (used for all 12 existing SSM secrets in `ssm.tf`) is insufficient here, or switch to that pattern to avoid introducing a new provider and the state-regeneration desync risk.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) as a new Terraform provider dependency with a single call site (the origin-guard value), while the codebase's established prior art for all 12 SSM secrets is `value = "CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` + operator CLI seeding (ssm.tf `rebar_secret_params`). No justification is given for why `random_password` is preferred over the existing operator-seeded pattern. The `.terraform.lock.hcl` confirms no `hashicorp/random` pin exists today. The new provider also carries a lifecycle risk: if the `random_password` resource is replaced (state taint, state loss, or provider upgrade), Terraform silently regenerates a new guard value and desynchronises the API Gateway static header from the SSM value the materialize script reads — whereas the CHANGEME-seed pattern is immune to this because Terraform never manages the value after seeding.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password` … the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the same pattern used for the other 12 SSM secrets: declare `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` in `ssm.tf` with `value = "CHANGEME"` and `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`, then seed it out-of-band with `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite --value "$(openssl rand -hex 32)"` in the deploy procedure. Read it back with a `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` source and pass the `value` attribute into the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` map — the same pattern `monitoring.tf` already uses for `alert_endpoint`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct or verify the claim that 'an HTTP API has no resource policy': check the AWS API Gateway HTTP API documentation for resource-based policy support, and either (a) update the plan's justification to accurately state that a resource policy is available but the identity-policy-only model is chosen as sufficient (with the reason), or (b) add a resource policy as a defense-in-depth layer scoped to the `rebar-opcert-admin` role ARN.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the architectural justification for restricting access purely via an identity-based inline policy on the IaC-created `rebar-opcert-admin` role. This is an unverified capability assertion that is factually incorrect: AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies (documented under 'Controlling access to HTTP APIs with AWS IAM' in the API Gateway developer guide). The assertion is load-bearing — it is the stated reason the plan omits a resource policy as a defense-in-depth layer — so if the claim is wrong, the choice was made on a false premise and an available second enforcement point was silently foreclosed.
findings.location: What section — 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy): `AWS_IAM` auth requires SigV4, and the IaC CREATES the admin role it restricts to'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an `aws_cloudwatch_log_group` for API Gateway access logs and wire it via `access_log_settings` on the API Gateway stage, plus at least one CloudWatch alarm (e.g., 5xx error rate) for the new HTTP API endpoint, consistent with the existing monitoring coverage in `monitoring.tf`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new internet-facing API Gateway HTTP API endpoint but specifies no access logging, no CloudWatch metrics/alarms, and no log group for it. API Gateway v2 access logs are disabled by default and require explicit `access_log_settings` configuration. The existing monitoring infrastructure (monitoring.tf, monitoring_s5.tf, etc.) covers EC2/Gerrit metrics but nothing covers the new API — leaving 4xx/5xx spikes, abuse patterns, and endpoint-down conditions invisible.
findings.location: What section / AC5: 'Deploy + prove' and Terraform declaration of the API Gateway HTTP API; no mention of access_log_settings, CloudWatch log group, or alarms for the API

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the claim 'an HTTP API has no resource policy': HTTP API v2 *does* support resource policies (aws_apigatewayv2_api `policy` attribute); rephrase as a deliberate design choice ('we omit a resource policy because identity-policy-based IAM is sufficient for this access pattern') rather than a capability statement.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the factual basis for the identity-policy-only access-control design (no aws_apigatewayv2_api `policy` attribute in IaC). This claim is Contradicted: AWS HTTP API v2 does support resource policies via the `policy` attribute on `aws_apigatewayv2_api` in the Terraform AWS provider. An executor who reads this as a hard capability constraint — rather than a deliberate design choice to omit a defense-in-depth layer — may be confused or may incorrectly conclude a resource policy is impossible when they encounter the attribute.
findings.location: What section: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with 'HTTP APIs support resource policies but we omit one here — identity-policy-only (execute-api:Invoke on the IaC-created role) is sufficient for this single-caller pattern and avoids the extra IaC surface.'

findings.finding: The leaf's AC4 uses `added_at_commit` as the era field name when pinning the deployed environment's public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, but the parent epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' section explicitly supersedes the earlier 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' schema: era boundaries are now `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions, operator-stamped by a `rebar trusted-env` helper), NOT `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` (main git SHAs). The leaf directly contradicts the parent's settled, superseding schema.
findings.location: AC4 / Leaf 'Deploy + prove' section; parent epic 6d0d 'Authoritative era anchor' section
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, change 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' to 'with `added_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log position, stamped by `rebar trusted-env`) per the parent epic's "Authoritative era anchor" resolution'. Update the 'Deploy + prove' bullet similarly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the ticket's `file_impact` metadata: replace `infra/opcert/` with `infra/terraform/` and update the reason to match the actual approach (API GW HTTP API + SSM params, not Fargate).
findings.finding: The ticket's file_impact metadata names `infra/opcert/` as the Terraform directory, but the plan body consistently specifies extending the EXISTING `infra/terraform/` module — these are different paths and the discrepancy means automated tooling keyed on file_impact would target the wrong directory.
findings.location: Ticket file_impact: {"path": "infra/opcert/", "reason": "Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager"} vs. What section: 'Terraform — extend the existing infra/terraform/'
findings.suggested_fix: Set file_impact path to `infra/terraform/` with reason `"Terraform: API Gateway HTTP API (v2) + rebar-opcert-admin IAM role + SSM SecureString parameters"`.

findings.finding: The API Gateway → EC2 nginx backend integration URI scheme (http:// vs https://) is undeclared. Without a VPC Link, API Gateway must reach the box over its public Elastic IP. The existing nginx configuration on :80 returns a 301 redirect for all non-ACME traffic (API Gateway HTTP integrations do not follow redirects), and :443 requires TLS with a matching certificate (rebar.solutions.navateam.com). The plan says 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' without specifying the integration URL, leaving the implementer to guess a detail that determines whether the integration works at all.
findings.location: What section: 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the /opcert/ routes'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/` as the integration URI so TLS works via SNI with the existing Let's Encrypt cert. Add an nginx rule that accepts the guard header check on :443 before proxying to the opcert container port.

findings.finding: The API Gateway → EC2 nginx integration URI scheme (http:// vs https://) is undeclared, but the two options have materially different behaviors given the network topology (no VPC Link means traffic crosses the public internet to the box's EIP). The existing nginx :80 block (confirmed in infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template) issues a 301 redirect for all non-ACME traffic; API Gateway HTTP_PROXY integration returns that 301 to the caller rather than following it, silently breaking all proxied requests. Using https://:443 works but requires API Gateway to validate the origin's Let's Encrypt cert (fine) — yet this is an implementation choice with a wrong-default trap the plan leaves unresolved.
findings.location: What section — 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the /opcert/ routes'
findings.suggested_fix: Use the origin-guard-protected loopback path directly: since API Gateway HTTP_PROXY cannot reach 127.0.0.1 on the remote box, the integration URI should target `https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/` (the box's public HTTPS endpoint with its valid LE cert), or alternatively have nginx proxy only on :443 for the /opcert/ location. State this explicitly in the What section.

findings.finding: The new materialize script (`infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`) is never wired into a boot orchestrator, and the plan does not specify a `systemctl reload nginx` step. The entire existing precedent — `materialize-deploy-key.sh` and `materialize-g2p-config.sh` — writes to container bind-mount dirs and is explicitly called from `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` before `docker compose up`. nginx is a HOST service (not in compose, confirmed in docker-compose.yml and compose-up.sh). Writing the guard value to an nginx conf include has no effect until nginx reloads. Without both (a) a call-site in compose-up.sh and (b) a `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx` in the script or the orchestrator, the origin-guard check is never enforced — a direct-to-origin request without the guard header would NOT be rejected (AC5 third check).
findings.location: What section — compose/nginx wiring; materialize script; AC3 and AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to compose-up.sh, immediately after the g2p config materialization block: `bash "${REPO_ROOT}/infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh"` — and have that script end with `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx` (fail-closed, matching the existing scripts' set -euo pipefail discipline). Document this in the What section.

findings.finding: The API Gateway → EC2 nginx integration URI scheme is unspecified, but the choice (http:// vs https://) is load-bearing: the existing nginx :80 server block issues a 301 redirect for all non-ACME traffic (confirmed in `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` lines 22-36), and AWS API Gateway HTTP_PROXY integrations do NOT follow 3xx redirects. If an implementer chooses `http://` (the natural reading of 'HTTP-integrated'), every proxied request receives a 301 and silently fails. The feasible path is https:// on port 443, but the plan never states this.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway HTTP API description: 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin'
findings.suggested_fix: If API Gateway reaches the box over its public Elastic IP, use `https://<domain>/opcert/` as the integration URI (the nginx :443 block proxies correctly). Alternatively, note that HTTP_PROXY without VPC Link hits the public interface — document the explicit URI scheme in the plan to avoid the http://→301 trap.

findings.finding: The plan says the API Gateway is 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the `/opcert/` routes' but never declares the integration URI scheme or port. The existing nginx config (`infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`) shows that port 80 issues a 301 redirect to HTTPS for all non-ACME traffic, so an `http://` integration URI pointing at port 80 would receive a redirect response rather than proxying to the opcert service. The integration must target `https://` on port 443 (or a loopback port where the opcert container is published), but the plan leaves this undeclared, making the integration URI unverified.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway paragraph: 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the `/opcert/` routes'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` follows the 'existing secret-materialization precedent' and will call the new `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`, but compose-up.sh is never mentioned as a file to modify. Inspection shows compose-up.sh EXPLICITLY calls each materialize script (line 177 calls `materialize-g2p-config.sh`); the new script will not be invoked unless compose-up.sh is also updated. This is an unenumerated consumer: the plan scopes 'the new script is `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`' but omits the call-site.
findings.location: What section: 'On the box the guard value is materialized from its SSM parameter…by a materialize script following the stack's existing secret-materialization precedent (infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh — SSM → file, same pattern; the new script is infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet in the Compose section of What: 'Extend `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` to call `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` before `docker compose up` (same pattern as the `materialize-g2p-config.sh` call at line 177).' And add a test assertion in AC3 that compose-up.sh invokes the new script.

findings.finding: infra/scripts/compose-up.sh is an unenumerated consumer that must be modified to call the new materialize-opcert-guard.sh script. Every existing materialize script (materialize-deploy-key.sh, materialize-g2p-config.sh) is explicitly called from compose-up.sh before `docker compose up` (lines 127 and 177). The new script follows the same pattern, but compose-up.sh is not listed as an edit target anywhere in the plan's 'What' section or Scope.
findings.location: What / Compose section; infra/scripts/compose-up.sh lines 127, 177
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Compose section: 'infra/scripts/compose-up.sh gains a call to infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh immediately before `docker compose up` (mirroring the materialize-g2p-config.sh invocation at line 177), with the same NON-FATAL wrapper pattern.'

findings.finding: `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` is the boot orchestrator that calls all existing materialize scripts (`materialize-deploy-key.sh`, `materialize-g2p-config.sh`) before `docker compose up`, but the plan does not specify that `compose-up.sh` must be updated to call the new `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`. Without this, the origin-guard value is never materialized into the nginx conf or service env on boot.
findings.location: What section: compose/nginx wiring; infra/scripts/compose-up.sh

findings.finding: infra/scripts/compose-up.sh is an unenumerated consumer that must be modified to call the new materialize-opcert-guard.sh script. Every existing materialize script (materialize-deploy-key.sh is called from infra/gerrit/setup-replication.sh; materialize-g2p-config.sh is called from compose-up.sh line 177) is wired into the boot/deploy orchestration flow. The new script at infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh will need a call site in compose-up.sh (before `docker compose up`) to materialize the guard value into the nginx conf at boot. The plan never names compose-up.sh as a file to be modified.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring; infra/scripts/compose-up.sh (line 177 area)
findings.suggested_fix: In the What/Compose section add: 'infra/scripts/compose-up.sh gains a pre-up call to materialize-opcert-guard.sh (following the materialize-g2p-config.sh call at line 177), materialized before docker compose up.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
